Re: Request for Advice Pursuant to Government Code Section 83114
Dear Ms. Donovan:
For the past several years, the Coastal Corunission has sustained significant
reductions in its annual budget from the state. Those reductions have
directly and negatively affected the Conunission's discharge of its
responsibilitiei. For example, because of travel costs, staff attendarrce at
oftsite meetings has been curtailed. Another significant result of the
budget reductions has been a virtual cessation of all training cpportunities
for conrnission staff. Generally, training sessions involve fees, and may
also necessitate travel. The Conrnission simply does not have enough funding
to cover these exPenses.
From time to time, however, other government agencies have expressed a
willingness to facilitate Cqunission staff participation in certain
activities. In addition, certain training opportunities sponsored by other
agencies have arisen. To assure that the Conmission and its officers and
"irpioy""" fu1ly "oreiy with the provisions of the Political Reform Act, the
Coastif Conrnission hereby reguests that the Fair Political Practices
Corrnission (FppC) issue writ€en advice pursuant to Government Code section
83114(b)withrespecttotheseactivitiesandqportunities.
Attachment I, enclosed, describes several situations which have occurred and
others which we expect to encounter in the future. In addition, that
;a-t;hma"[ iaentii'i.J-""r"ra1 specific questions regarding these situations,
as welr as several guestions regarding Che FPPC's opinion in stgne, (1977) 3
FppC ops. 52. Vib have also enclosed a general description of the
Conrnission's activities and staff. (at[aclrnent II.) 
We request that the
FppC include in its opinion a discussion of the questions set forth in the
attachment, as well aL any other relevant issues, and that the advice
identify any effects on the Conrnission's officers and erployees, as well as
any etfects on the Conunission or its decisions. Because of the upconning

ATTACHMET'II I I
GENERAL BACKGROUND
The California Coastal Conrnission carries out various responsibi l it ies
relating to the managernenb and developinent of coastal zone resources.
within specified areas, the Conrnission reviews permit applications for
developrnent proposals. The Comnission also reviews 1oca1 coastal programs
subnitted by local governments, long range development plans submitted by
universities, and port master plans. As a general matter, after
certification of a program for a particular area, some of the Conrnission's
permit review authority is delegated to the local goverrment. The
Conrnission retains original permit iurisdiction with respect to development
in certain areas, e.g., state vraters. In addition, in specified areas, and
for various types of projects, certain local permitting decisions are
subject to appellate review by the cormission. The cormission also c:onducts
enforcement activities relating to these functions. Cormission staff
activities include the review and analysis of each of the types of proposals
described above, and the preparation of recormendations and proposed
findings for the Ccnrnission's consideration.
The comnission is the state's designated agency for purposes of the federal
Coastal zone Management Act (16 U.S.C. section 1451 et seq.) zursuant to
this designation, the Corunission reviews consistency determinations for
projects to be carried out by the federal goverrment, and consistency
terlifications for activities subject to federal permitting requirements,
including plans for oi1 and gas exploration on the outer continental shelf.
Again, staff's role is to review all proposals, and to prepare
recormendations and proposed findings for the Comission.
The comnission and its staff are also normally involved in various related
activities. For exarnple, comission staff prepare various informational
documents, such as a lununary of oil and gas activities affecting the coastal
zone, and a newsletter for 1ocal goverrunents, . cormission staff participate
on r;view panels created to prepare environrnental iflpact reports or
statements, and on conmittees researching or addressi ng. various . coastal
f6;;: 
C6rrnission staff nay also attend various training sessions related
to their work. Both Conrnissioners and Conrnission staff provide
informational services, including written and oral presentations'
the Cormission's staff is largely conprised of coastal analysts. several
specialists are included. For example, the comnission's staff includes an
"'.,gin"ut, a marine biologist, and a geo1ogist... In general, the Cormission
stitf inctuaes only one specialist in each subject ratter area' The
conrnission's staff also includes executive staff, attorneys, a&ninistrative
personnel, support staff, and interns-
The california coastal Act is set forth in the california Public Resources
code, beginning at section 30000. The conrnission's regulations are set
forth in title 14 of the california code of Regulations, beginning at
section 13001.

subnitted by local governments, long range development plans submitted by
universities, and port master plans. As a general matter, after
certification of a program for a particular area, some of the Conrnission's
permit review authority is delegated to the local goverrment. The
Conrnission retains original permit iurisdiction with respect to development
in certain areas, e.g., state vraters. In addition, in specified areas, and
for various types of projects, certain local permitting decisions are
subject to appellate review by the cormission. The cormission also c:onducts
enforcement activities relating to these functions. Cormission staff
activities include the review and analysis of each of the types of proposals
described above, and the preparation of recormendations and proposed
findings for the Ccnrnission's consideration.
The comnission is the state's designated agency for purposes of the federal
Coastal zone Management Act (16 U.S.C. section 1451 et seq.) zursuant to
this designation, the Corunission reviews consistency determinations for
projects to be carried out by the federal goverrment, and consistency
terlifications for activities subject to federal permitting requirements,
including plans for oi1 and gas exploration on the outer continental shelf.
Again, staff's role is to review all proposals, and to prepare
recormendations and proposed findings for the Comission.
The comnission and its staff are also normally involved in various related
activities. For exarnple, comission staff prepare various informational
documents, such as a lununary of oil and gas activities affecting the coastal
zone, and a newsletter for 1ocal goverrunents, . cormission staff participate
on r;view panels created to prepare environrnental iflpact reports or
statements, and on conmittees researching or addressi ng. various . coastal
f6;;: 
C6rrnission staff nay also attend various training sessions related
to their work. Both Conrnissioners and Conrnission staff provide
informational services, including written and oral presentations'
the Cormission's staff is largely conprised of coastal analysts. several
specialists are included. For example, the comnission's staff includes an
"'.,gin"ut, a marine biologist, and a geo1ogist... In general, the Cormission
stitf inctuaes only one specialist in each subject ratter area' The
conrnission's staff also includes executive staff, attorneys, a&ninistrative

The california coastal Act is set forth in the california Public Resources
code, beginning at section 30000. The conrnission's regulations are set
forth in title 14 of the california code of Regulations, beginning at
